name: Milk (Cow) (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fresh whole cow milk (3.25% milkfat)

components.name: lactose
components.source: USDA FDC 746782 (Foundation, 24 samples) / FDC 172217 (SR Legacy 5.05 g)
components.evidence: DOMINANT carbon/energy source of milk; the only quantitatively significant free/disaccharide sugar (beta-1,4-galactosyl-glucose)

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: Milk composition literature (free glucose ~10-20 mg/100 mL fresh cow milk); USDA reports 0 for the disaccharide-only panel
components.evidence: Trace free monosaccharide from partial lactose turnover; USDA lists total-sugars as lactose only, so free glucose added from HPLC literature

components.name: D-galactose
components.source: Milk composition literature (free galactose ~10-20 mg/100 g fresh milk)
components.evidence: Trace free monosaccharide; readily fermentable; not resolved in the USDA lactose-only panel

components.name: citric acid (citrate)
components.source: Variability of citric acid in cow milk (ResearchGate 304771887): mean 133 mg/100 mL, range 95-203 mg/100 mL
components.evidence: KEY OMISSION FIX — citrate is the DOMINANT organic acid of milk (~1.3-2.0 g/L) and was absent from current_components; major secondary carbon source and Ca-buffer

components.name: L-lactate (lactic acid)
components.source: Dairy organic-acid HPLC literature (Marsili 1981; Fernandez-Garcia & McGregor 1994, ScienceDirect S0021967304001311)
components.evidence: Low in FRESH milk (~30-60 mg/L); rises sharply on bacterial fermentation. Already present in current list; amount added

components.name: orotic acid (orotate)
components.source: PubMed 1958838 / Agrarforschung Schweiz 2008: ~66 mg/L (6.6 mg/100 g), seasonal 40-100 mg/L
components.evidence: Pyrimidine-pathway organic acid characteristic of ruminant milk; already listed, amount added

components.name: uric acid (urate)
components.source: Dairy organic-acid HPLC literature (Marsili 1981): ~50-70 mg/L raw milk
components.evidence: Purine-catabolism organic acid; present in current list, amount added

components.name: hippuric acid
components.source: Dairy organic-acid HPLC literature (Marsili 1981; ScienceDirect S0021967304001311): ~30-50 mg/L, higher on forage diet
components.evidence: Benzoate-glycine conjugate from bovine metabolism; MISSING from current_components, added

components.name: pyruvate (pyruvic acid)
components.source: Dairy organic-acid HPLC literature (~10-20 mg/L raw milk)
components.evidence: Trace; present in current list, amount added

components.name: acetate (acetic acid)
components.source: Raw-milk short-chain fatty acid GC-MS (PMC10093571) and HPLC literature
components.evidence: Trace free acetate; rises with microbial activity

components.name: formate
components.evidence: Trace, present in current list; not reliably quantified in fresh milk

components.name: L-malate
components.evidence: Trace TCA-cycle acid; present in current list

components.name: succinate
components.evidence: Trace TCA-cycle acid; present in current list

components.name: protein (total)
components.evidence: ~80% casein, ~20% whey; principal nitrogen/amino-acid source

components.name: L-glutamate (Glx)
components.source: USDA FDC 172217 amino-acid panel; corroborated PMC8535404
components.evidence: Most abundant amino acid residue (casein-derived)

components.name: L-proline
components.evidence: Casein-rich residue

components.name: L-leucine
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-lysine
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-valine
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-isoleucine
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-phenylalanine
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-threonine
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-histidine
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-methionine
components.evidence: Essential, sulfur amino acid

components.name: L-tryptophan
components.evidence: Essential amino acid

components.name: L-cystine
components.evidence: Sulfur amino acid (whey-rich)

components.name: total fat
components.evidence: Milkfat; ~98% triacylglycerol

components.name: saturated fatty acids (total)
components.evidence: Dominant lipid class in milkfat

components.name: palmitic acid (16:0)
components.evidence: Most abundant single fatty acid

components.name: short/medium-chain saturated FA (4:0-12:0)
components.evidence: Characteristic ruminant short-chain FA (butyrate, caproate, caprylate, caprate, laurate)

components.name: monounsaturated fatty acids (mainly oleic 18:1)
components.evidence: Predominantly oleic acid

components.name: polyunsaturated fatty acids
components.evidence: Linoleic + alpha-linolenic

components.name: cholesterol
components.evidence: Genuine animal sterol of milk (KEEP — plausible for this animal food)

components.name: calcium
components.evidence: Largely colloidal (casein-micelle) + soluble citrate/phosphate salts

components.name: potassium
components.evidence: Major cation

components.name: chloride
components.evidence: Major anion; present in current list, amount added

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Very low; milk is iron-poor

components.name: iodine
components.source: Milk composition literature / McCance & Widdowson (~15-30 ug/100 g, feed-dependent)
components.evidence: Milk is a major dietary iodine source; not in USDA panel, added

components.name: riboflavin (vitamin B2)
components.evidence: Milk is a leading dietary source of B2

components.name: vitamin B6 (pyridoxine group)
components.evidence: Sum of pyridoxal/pyridoxamine/pyridoxine forms already listed

components.name: niacin (vitamin B3)
components.evidence: Low preformed niacin (tryptophan supplies more)

components.name: vitamin B12 (cobalamin)
components.evidence: Milk is a significant dietary B12 source

components.name: biotin (vitamin B7)
components.source: Milk composition literature (~1-4 ug/100 g); not in USDA panel
components.evidence: Present in current list, amount added

components.name: vitamin A (retinol activity equiv.)
components.evidence: Mostly retinol in milkfat; small beta-carotene

components.name: vitamin D (cholecalciferol)
components.source: USDA FDC 172217 (0.1 ug natural); fortified milk ~1.3 ug (FDC 746782)
components.evidence: Natural level low; fortified whole milk carries ~1.1-1.3 ug/100 g

components.name: vitamin C (ascorbate)
components.source: USDA FDC 172217 (0.0 mg); trace ~0.9 mg in very fresh raw milk
components.evidence: Near-zero in processed milk; present in current list

components.name: choline
components.evidence: Mostly phosphocholine/glycerophosphocholine

components.name: myo-inositol
components.evidence: Free cyclitol; present in current list, amount added

components.name: urea
components.source: Milk composition literature (milk urea nitrogen; ~20-35 mg/100 g)
components.evidence: Principal non-protein nitrogen; usable N source; present in current list

components.name: N-acetylneuraminate (sialic acid)
components.evidence: Bound largely in oligosaccharides/glycoproteins; genuine milk component

remove.name: Arsenate
remove.reason: non-nutritive toxic trace element / contaminant, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Tetracycline
remove.reason: veterinary antibiotic residue/contaminant, not a milk nutrient

remove.name: Estradiol c
remove.reason: steroid hormone present only at ng/L trace; FooDB predicted, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Estriol c
remove.reason: steroid hormone at ng/L trace; FooDB predicted, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Estrone cytosol
remove.reason: steroid hormone at ng/L trace; FooDB predicted, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Dehydroepiandrosterone
remove.reason: steroid hormone at ultratrace; not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Serotonin
remove.reason: trace bioamine; not a quantitative growth nutrient

remove.name: Pectin C6H7O6
remove.reason: plant cell-wall polysaccharide; biologically absent in milk, FooDB matrix error

remove.name: Gallic acid
remove.reason: plant polyphenol; negligible/implausible in milk, FooDB noise

remove.name: Toluene
remove.reason: volatile hydrocarbon contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Calcitriol
remove.reason: active 1,25-vitamin-D hormone at ultratrace; redundant with vitamin D (cholecalciferol)

remove.name: 25-Hydroxyvitamin D2
remove.reason: trace vitamin-D metabolite form; redundant with vitamin D entry

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: ultratrace non-essential element; contaminant-level, not a growth nutrient

missing_added: citric acid (citrate) — dominant organic acid of milk, was entirely absent from current_components
hippuric acid — bovine-metabolite organic acid, absent
free D-glucose and D-galactose with amounts (USDA panel lists lactose only)
quantitative amounts for lactose, all amino acids, vitamins, minerals, and fatty acids
keep_uncovered: Beta-Carotene, Lutein, Zeaxanthin, Echinenone (genuine milkfat carotenoids)
Glycine betaine, Taurine, L-carnitine and acylcarnitines (genuine minor milk metabolites)
milk triacylglycerols TG(...) and odd/branched-chain fatty acids (pentadecanoic, heptadecanoic, vaccenic) — genuine ruminant milkfat lipids
orotate/urate/allantoin and nucleosides (genuine milk purine/pyrimidine metabolites)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 172217 (SR Legacy: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, without added vit A/D) — full vitamin/mineral/amino-acid/fatty-acid panel
USDA FoodData Central FDC 746782 (Foundation Foods: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D; 24 samples, 2018)
Variability of citric acid in cow milk composition (ResearchGate 304771887): mean citric acid 133 mg/100 mL
Orotic acid in cow milk: PubMed 1958838 / Agrarforschung Schweiz 2008 (~66 mg/L)
Amino acid composition of cow milk: PMC8535404
Dairy organic-acid HPLC methodology and typical raw-milk values: Marsili et al. 1981 J. Dairy Sci.; Fernandez-Garcia & McGregor 1994 (ScienceDirect S0021967304001311); raw-milk SCFA GC-MS PMC10093571
notes: Dominant carbon/energy source is LACTOSE (~4.8 g/100 g, ~140 mM); the key correction is adding CITRIC ACID/citrate (~0.16 g/100g, ~8 mM), the dominant milk organic acid that was entirely missing, plus free glucose/galactose, hippuric acid, iodine and biotin. Lactic acid is LOW in fresh milk (rises on fermentation). Removed heavy-metal contaminants (arsenate, cadmium, nickel), an antibiotic residue (tetracycline), ng/L steroid hormones, plant-only compounds (pectin, gallic acid), and a volatile contaminant (toluene). Cholesterol and milkfat sterols/carotenoids KEPT as genuine animal-food components.
